title: Currant Chelsea buns
subtitle: Sticky yeasted spiral buns with currants and mixed peel
excerpt: Chelsea buns in the eighteenth-century Bun House tradition: a soft yeasted dough rolled with butter, brown sugar, mixed spice, currants and mixed peel, sliced into spirals, and baked close together so they pull apart in squares. Twelve buns, finished with a sticky sugar glaze.
sourceNotes: Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg: documents the eighteenth-century Bun House at Chelsea where the bun was popularised. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: documents the household form with currants, peel and mixed spice. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the sticky-sugar glaze applied warm.

recipe.freezeNotes: Bake, cool, freeze in a tin; refresh in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cold-proof the shaped buns overnight in the fridge; bake from cold, adding 3 minutes.

recipeTools.notes: 23×23 cm or 25 cm round — the buns proof and bake together, touching.

content.text: Chelsea buns are an eighteenth-century London bake: a yeasted dough enriched with butter and egg, rolled out, spread with butter and brown sugar, scattered with currants, mixed peel and mixed spice, rolled into a long sausage, cut into 12 spirals, and baked in a tin together so they swell against each other and pull apart in soft, sticky squares.
The defining finish is the sticky glaze: a sugar-and-water syrup brushed over the buns straight from the oven while they are hot. The syrup cools to a soft sticky shell.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{yeast-fast-action}} in a stand-mixer bowl. Add lukewarm {{whole-milk}}, softened first {{unsalted-butter}} and the first {{eggs}}. Mix on low for 7 minutes to a soft, smooth dough. Bulk ferment in an oiled bowl at room temperature for 1 hour until doubled.

Knock back the dough and roll on a floured surface to a 40×30 cm rectangle. Spread evenly with the softened second {{unsalted-butter}}. Scatter the {{soft-brown-sugar}} and {{mixed-spice}} across, then the {{dried-currants}} and {{mixed-peel}}. From a long edge, roll up tightly into a sausage. Slice into 12 pieces with a sharp knife. Set cut-side-up in the lined square tin, 4 across and 3 down, with a small gap between each — they will spread and meet.

Proof in the warm for 90 minutes until visibly puffed and the buns are touching. Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Bake for 22–25 minutes; the tops should be deep gold and the buns should sound hollow when tapped on top. While the buns bake, dissolve the second {{caster-sugar}} in the {{water}} in a small pan over a low heat and bring to a simmer for 2 minutes. As soon as the buns come out, brush the sugar syrup generously over the tops. Cool 10 minutes in the tin before lifting out.
